sourceSummary.oneLine: This IEC 60601-1 excerpt provides rationale for electrical safety requirements including means of protection, applied parts, leakage currents, dielectric strength, creepage/clearance, protective earthing, mains isolation, and mechanical hazards.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Supports regulatory safety files, test planning, design justification, risk management, and standards traceability for medical electrical equipment under IEC 60601-1.
keyPoints: MOPP and MOOP are distinguished because patient contact conditions require a larger safety margin than operator contact conditions.
Y capacitors used across insulation barriers are safety critical and must be certified and monitored to IEC 60384-14 by a recognized test house.

Defibrillation-proof applied parts are tested so accessible parts do not deliver hazardous charge/energy and equipment continues to meet basic safety and essential performance after defibrillation exposure.
Protective earth connections require impedance and current-carrying capability checks because weak connections may not be detected by resistance measurement alone.
Leakage current limits are justified by ventricular fibrillation, tissue necrosis, perceptibility, and burn risk considerations, with special limits for Type B, BF, and CF applied parts.
Creepage, clearance, dielectric strength, and thin sheet insulation requirements are aligned in part with IEC 60950-1, IEC 60664-1, and prior IEC 60601-1 editions.

entities.name: MANUFACTURER
entities.evidence: The manufacturer determines which kind of part the means of protection will protect and may choose MOOP routes or MOPP treatment.

entities.name: PATIENT
entities.evidence: The rationale repeatedly distinguishes patient safety, applied parts, patient connections, and patient leakage current from operator protection.

entities.name: OPERATOR
entities.evidence: The excerpt explains that residual risk for operator contact is acceptable under MOOP assumptions but patient exposure requires a larger safety margin.

entities.name: RESPONSIBLE ORGANIZATION
entities.evidence: Leakage current measurement is described as enabling periodic checking by the responsible organization.

entities.name: recognized test house
entities.evidence: Y capacitors must be certified and monitored by a recognised test house to IEC 60384-14.

entities.name: National Committees
entities.evidence: National Committee comments and responses are cited for requirements on insulation, patient connectors, and fuses/over-current releases.

concepts.name: Means of Patient Protection (MOPP)
concepts.evidence: Patient safety receives retained IEC 60601-1 second-edition values and higher margins than operator protection.

concepts.name: Means of Operator Protection (MOOP)
concepts.evidence: For MOOP, the manufacturer may apply IEC 60950-1 insulation coordination, simplified table values, or treat MOOP as MOPP.

concepts.name: Y capacitors
concepts.evidence: Y capacitors bridge insulation for interference suppression and are safety critical because they provide leakage paths to earth or across barriers.

concepts.name: F-type applied parts
concepts.evidence: The essential feature of an F-type applied part is separation from other parts and patient leads must prevent accidental patient-to-earth or mains connections.

concepts.name: Defibrillation-proof applied parts
concepts.evidence: The marking indicates an applied part can remain attached during defibrillation without adverse effect on subsequent equipment use.

concepts.name: Protective earthing
concepts.evidence: Protective earth connections are addressed for terminals, moving parts, impedance, fault-current capability, and potential equalization.

concepts.name: Leakage current
concepts.evidence: The excerpt explains allowable earth leakage, touch current, patient leakage current, patient auxiliary current, and measurement circuits.

concepts.name: Creepage distance and air clearance
concepts.evidence: Values are based on overvoltage categories, IEC 60950-1, IEC 60664-1, IEC 61010-1, and IEC 60601-1 patient-protection tables.

concepts.name: Mechanical hazards
concepts.evidence: Clause 9 addresses mechanical hazards from moving parts, rough surfaces, sharp edges, instability, expelled parts, vibration, noise, supports, and suspensions.

evidence.claim: IEC 60601-1 distinguishes MOPP and MOOP because patient exposure conditions justify a higher safety margin than operator exposure conditions.
evidence.evidenceText: The working group decided that a larger margin of safety should be applied where PATIENT safety is concerned, while operator residual risk under IEC 60664-1/IEC 60950-1 assumptions was acceptable.

evidence.claim: For MOOP, the manufacturer has three compliance options: IEC 60950-1 insulation coordination, simplified table values derived from IEC 60950-1, or treating the protection as MOPP.
evidence.evidenceText: The revision allows the MANUFACTURER three options: apply IEC 60950-1, apply table values derived from IEC 60950-1, or treat MOOP as MOPP.

evidence.claim: Y capacitors used for interference suppression across insulation barriers are safety critical and require IEC 60384-14 certification/monitoring by a recognized test house.
evidence.evidenceText: Y capacitors provide leakage paths to earth or across a barrier, so they must be certified and monitored by a recognised test house to IEC 60384-14.

evidence.claim: A Y1 capacitor can provide two MOOPs but only one MOPP; a Y2 capacitor can provide one MOOP only.
evidence.evidenceText: One Y1 capacitor can be used to provide two MOOPs but only one MOPP; a Y2 capacitor can be used to provide one MOOP only.

evidence.claim: For MOPP barriers in the 212 V peak to 354 V peak range, two MOPP require 4,000 V a.c. testing, making Y1 capacitors necessary in the stated example.
evidence.evidenceText: For voltages from 212 V peak to 354 V peak, two MOPP are tested at 4,000 V a.c.; two Y2 capacitors in series would not withstand this voltage.

evidence.claim: The essential feature of an F-type applied part is separation from other parts, and the standard quantifies the necessary degree of separation.
evidence.evidenceText: The essential feature of an F-TYPE APPLIED PART is its separation from other parts; the subclause specifies and quantifies the necessary degree of separation.

evidence.claim: Patient lead and cable connector requirements address accidental patient-to-earth connection and accidental connection to equipment or conductive parts that could exceed allowable leakage current.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale identifies two hazards: accidental patient-to-earth connection via detached leads and accidental patient connection to parts from which excessive leakage current could flow.

evidence.claim: IEC 60601-1 uses 250 V as the assumed external voltage for internally powered ME equipment and for equipment with highest rated supply voltage below 100 V.
evidence.evidenceText: For internally powered ME equipment the specified value remains 250 V; if highest rated supply voltage is less than 100 V, the external voltage assumed for relevant tests is 250 V.

evidence.claim: Defibrillation-proof applied part testing covers both hazardous charge/energy on accessible parts and continued basic safety and essential performance after defibrillation exposure.
evidence.evidenceText: Tests ensure non-earthed accessible parts will not deliver hazardous charge or energy and that ME equipment continues to function for basic safety and essential performance after defibrillation voltage.

evidence.claim: If accompanying documents do not state a recovery time after defibrillation exposure, the equipment must recover and deliver basic safety and essential performance immediately.
evidence.evidenceText: There is no requirement to state recovery time, but if absent the ME EQUIPMENT has to recover and deliver BASIC SAFETY and ESSENTIAL PERFORMANCE immediately.

evidence.claim: In the absence of a relevant particular standard, IEC 60601-1 specifies 5 kV d.c. as the defibrillation-proof applied part test voltage.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale states the equipment must withstand full no-load defibrillation voltage and specifies 5 kV d.c. as the appropriate test voltage absent a relevant particular standard.
evidence.pageTarget: p. 268, Rationale for impulse test voltage

evidence.claim: Protective earth connections relevant only to operator safety may comply with IEC 60601-1 or IEC 60950-1, but IEC 60950-1 is not allowed where both operator and patient safety are relevant.
evidence.evidenceText: Protective earth connections only relevant to operators may comply with either standard; this alternative is not allowed when relevant to both operators and patients.

evidence.claim: Protective earth connections require checks of both impedance and current-carrying capability because resistance measurement alone may miss weak spots.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale states it is necessary to check both impedance and current-carrying capability, and that overheating weak spots might not be discovered by resistance measurement alone.

evidence.claim: Class II ME equipment may have a protective-earth connection for functional reasons only, provided this does not degrade electric shock protection.
evidence.evidenceText: The requirement allows Class II ME equipment to have a protective earth connection for functional reasons only and states the allowance does not degrade protection against electric shock.

evidence.claim: Type CF applied parts are suitable for direct cardiac applications with regard to leakage current, while Type B and BF are generally suitable for patient contact excluding the heart.
evidence.evidenceText: TYPE B and TYPE BF applied parts are generally suitable for external or internal contact excluding the heart; TYPE CF applied parts are suitable for direct cardiac applications with regard to leakage current.

evidence.claim: Supply mains frequencies of 50 Hz and 60 Hz fall in the frequency range of highest risk for ventricular fibrillation.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale states risk is highest and approximately equal for 10 Hz to 200 Hz, and that 50 Hz and 60 Hz mains frequencies are in the range of highest risk.

evidence.claim: Earth leakage current is not a hazard per se, but excessive values can affect installation earthing systems and current-imbalance circuit breakers.
evidence.evidenceText: The patient and operator are protected by patient leakage and touch-current limits, but excessive earth leakage current could pose problems for earthing systems and current-imbalance breakers.

evidence.claim: For Type CF applied parts, normal-condition patient leakage current of 10 μA is justified as readily achievable and not significantly increasing ventricular fibrillation risk.
evidence.evidenceText: The allowable patient leakage current for Type CF applied parts in normal condition is 10 μA, with probability 0.002 for causing ventricular fibrillation or pump failure.

evidence.claim: Total patient leakage current limits are necessary because multiple functions or applied parts can make total leakage much higher than a single function or patient connection.
evidence.evidenceText: The standard's values are for a single function or connection; with multiple functions or applied parts the total patient leakage current could be much higher, requiring total limits.

evidence.claim: IEC 60601-1 leakage-current measurement may use an isolating transformer, but in high-input-power equipment or ME systems this may be infeasible and measurement without one can read higher.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale says an isolation transformer is not absolutely necessary, may be infeasible for high-input-power ME equipment and ME systems, and measurements without it can be greater.

evidence.claim: IEC 60990 measuring devices for general use are not appropriate for patient leakage current measurement under this standard.
evidence.evidenceText: Although IEC 60990 specifies measuring devices for general use, none would be appropriate for measuring patient leakage current.

evidence.claim: For dielectric strength, Table 6 uses IEC 60950-1 values for operator protection and second-edition IEC 60601-1 values for patient protection.
evidence.evidenceText: In Table 6, values for operator protection are taken from IEC 60950-1 and values for patient protection are taken from the second edition of IEC 60601-1.

evidence.claim: For the common 220 V to 240 V r.m.s. working-voltage range, IEC 60601-1 retained a 4,000 V r.m.s. dielectric test voltage to align with the second edition.
evidence.evidenceText: To align with the second edition for 220 V r.m.s to 240 V r.m.s., the test voltage of 4,000 V r.m.s. was retained.

evidence.claim: Creepage and clearance values for MOOP use IEC 60950-1 principles derived from IEC 60664-1, while MOPP tables are taken from second-edition IEC 60601-1.
evidence.evidenceText: The tables for MOOPs use IEC 60950-1 values based on IEC 60664-1 principles; MOPP tables are taken from the second edition of IEC 60601-1.

evidence.claim: For defibrillation-proof applied parts, IEC 60664-1:2007 Table 2 F.2 supports 4 mm spacing for 5 kV pulses shorter than 10 ms.
evidence.evidenceText: A distance of 4 mm is adequate for pulses of 5 kV having a short duration of less than 10 ms, such as defibrillator voltages.

evidence.claim: Internal wiring with only one securing means that could allow a loosened or broken wire to create a hazardous situation is not compliant with single fault condition expectations.
evidence.evidenceText: Connections with only one means of being secured that would prevent a loosened/broken wire from creating a hazard are considered not in compliance.

evidence.claim: Mains isolation switches intended to isolate from the supply mains retain a 3 mm minimum contact separation requirement for prudence and alignment with IEC 60601-1 second edition, IEC 60065, and IEC 60950-1.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale stayed with the 3 mm requirement for all switches intended to provide isolation from the supply mains and cites consistency with IEC 60065 and IEC 60950-1.

evidence.claim: Clause 9 covers mechanical hazards including moving parts, rough surfaces, sharp edges, instability, expelled parts, vibration, noise, and breakdown of patient supports and suspension means.
evidence.evidenceText: Clause 9 requirements describe mechanical hazards caused by moving parts, rough surfaces, sharp edges and corners, instability, expelled parts, vibration and noise, and breakdown of patient supports and suspensions.

evidence.claim: Moving-part mechanical hazard controls may include distance, restricted access, barriers, risk reduction, operator control, or backup systems to achieve acceptable residual risk.
evidence.evidenceText: The rationale lists protection by sufficient distance, restricted access, barriers, reducing risk, adequate operator control, and backup systems achieving acceptable residual risk.

evidence.claim: Motion control systems with the operator in the feedback loop need continuous activation, such as momentary contact or dead-man switches.
evidence.evidenceText: Motion control systems with the OPERATOR in the feedback loop need to employ continuous activation, with speed of motion and visible feedback also adequate.

reviewFlags.reason: This chunk is primarily Annex A rationale text rather than the normative clause text; extracted claims should be treated as rationale-supported unless the evidence explicitly states a requirement.
The source metadata authoritySignal is FDA, but this chunk itself supports IEC as the issuing standards authority and does not mention FDA.
OCR artifacts are present, including duplicated page licensing text and occasional typographic errors such as 'PAIENT' and inconsistent amendment wording.
